coredata.dc:title: Coupling of chrysanthemum-shaped cobalt hydroxide and nitrogen-doped carbon dots for high-performance hybrid supercapacitors
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Zihan
Zheng, Weijie
Zhang, Xiang
Zheng, Yuying
coredata.dc:description: Cobalt hydroxide is expecting electrode material for high-performance supercapacitors, however, it is still a challenge to improve its lack of transmission track and active sites. An effective method to improve the electrochemistry performance of cobalt hydroxide is to couple it with graphene quantum dots. Herein, we report the preparation of Cobalt hydroxide/N doped graphene quantum dots (Co(OH)2/NQDs) composites and study the electrochemistry performance of Co(OH)2/NQDs composites as supercapacitors electrode materials. Benefit from the strong coupling between NQDs and Co(OH)2 creates new electron transfer channels (Co-N). The hybrid supercapacitor device based on Co(OH)2/NQDs composite and GA shows a high energy density of about 33.6 Wh kg−1 at a power density of 800 Wk g−1, and it retains about 137% specific capacitances after 8000 cycle test. Due to the negative potential of NQDS, it can generate an electrostatic reaction with Co2+, which can stabilize cobalt hydroxide after long cycles.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Supercapacitors
Cobalt hydroxide
Graphene
Storage

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XRD, (b) Raman, (c) FT-IR, and (d)TG of NQDs, Co(OH)2, and Co(OH)2/NQDs-3.
SEM images of (a) Co(OH)2, (b) Co(OH)2/NQDs-1, (c) Co(OH)2/NQDs-2, (d) Co(OH)2/NQDs-3, (e) Co(OH)2/NQDs-4, and (f) Zeta potential of NQDs.
(a) SEM image, (b, c) TEM images, (d) HRTEM image, (b1) SAED pattern, and (d–j) EDX overlap and element mapping profiles of the Co(OH)2/NQDs- 3 sample.
(a) Full-scan XPS spectra of Co(OH)2, Co(OH)2/NQDs-3, and the pure NQDs materials. (b) High-resolution Co 2p of Co(OH)2 and Co(OH)2/NQDs-4. (c) N 1 s spectra of NQDs and Co(OH)2/NQDs-3.

(a) CV curves of Co(OH)2 and Co(OH)2/NQDs at the scan rate of 20 mV s−1; (b) Charge-discharge profile of Co(OH)2 and Co(OH)2/NQDs at the current density of 1 A/g; (c) Charge-discharge profile of Co(OH)2/NQDs-3 at various current densities; (d) The specific capacitance values of Co(OH)2 and Co(OH)2/NQDs calculated by the discharge curves.
CV curves at various current densities of the Co(OH)2/NQDs-3 electrode.

(a) CV curves at various current densities of the Co(OH)2 electrode, (b) CV curves at various current densities of the Co(OH)2/NQDs-1 electrode, (c) CV curves at various current densities of the Co(OH)2/NQDs-2 electrode, (d) CV curves at various current densities of the Co(OH)2/NQDs-4 electrode.

(a) GCD curves at various current densities of the Co(OH)2 electrode, (b) GCD curves at various current densities of the Co(OH)2/NQDs-1 electrode, (c) GCD curves at various current densities of the Co(OH)2/NQDs-2 electrode, (d) GCD curves at various current densities of the Co(OH)2/NQDs-4 electrode.
(a) CV curves at various scan rates of GA, and (b) GCD curves at various current densities of the GA electrode.

GCD curves of Co(OH)2/NQDs-3//GA hybrid supercapacitor device at different current densities before the cycling test; (b) GCD curves of Co(OH)2/NQDs-3 //GA hybrid supercapacitor device at different current densities after cycling test; (c) Cycling stability of the device at a current density of 1 A/g. (d) The specific capacitances of the device calculated by the discharge curves;
Ragone plot of the Co(OH)2/NQDs-3//GA hybrid supercapacitor device.
Co 2p, N 1 s spectra of the Co(OH)2/NQDs-3 after cycles test.
O 1 s spectra of the Co(OH)2/NQDs-3 before and after cycles test.

head.ce:title: Coupling of chrysanthemum-shaped cobalt hydroxide and nitrogen-doped carbon dots for high-performance hybrid supercapacitors

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Materials Science and Engineering, Fuzhou University, Fuzhou 350116, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Materials Science and Engineering, Fuzhou University, Fuzhou 350116, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: CAS Key Laboratory of Design and Assembly of Functional Nanostructures, and Fujian Provincial Key Laboratory of Nanomaterials, State Key Laboratory of Structural Chemistry, Key Laboratory of Optoelectronic Materials Chemistry and Physics, Fujian Institute of Research on the Structure of Matter, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Fuzhou, Fujian 350002, PR China

State Key Laboratory of Structural Chemistry
Key Laboratory of Optoelectronic Materials Chemistry and Physics
Fujian Institute of Research on the Structure of Matter

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: CAS Key Laboratory of Design and Assembly of Functional Nanostructures, and Fujian Provincial Key Laboratory of Nanomaterials, State Key Laboratory of Structural Chemistry, Key Laboratory of Optoelectronic Materials Chemistry and Physics, Fujian Institute of Research on the Structure of Matter, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Fuzhou, Fujian 350002, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: National Engineering Research Center of Chemical Fertilizer Catalyst, College of Chemical Engineering, Fuzhou University, Fuzhou 350002, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National Engineering Research Center of Chemical Fertilizer Catalyst

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: National Engineering Research Center of Chemical Fertilizer Catalyst, College of Chemical Engineering, Fuzhou University, Fuzhou 350002, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Cobalt hydroxide is expecting electrode material for high-performance supercapacitors, however, it is still a challenge to improve its lack of transmission track and active sites. An effective method to improve the electrochemistry performance of cobalt hydroxide is to couple it with graphene quantum dots. Herein, we report the preparation of Cobalt hydroxide/N doped graphene quantum dots (Co(OH)2/NQDs) composites and study the electrochemistry performance of Co(OH)2/NQDs composites as supercapacitors electrode materials. Benefit from the strong coupling between NQDs and Co(OH)2 creates new electron transfer channels (Co-N). The hybrid supercapacitor device based on Co(OH)2/NQDs composite and GA shows a high energy density of about 33.6 Wh kg−1 at a power density of 800 Wk g−1, and it retains about 137% specific capacitances after 8000 cycle test. Due to the negative potential of NQDS, it can generate an electrostatic reaction with Co2+, which can stabilize cobalt hydroxide after long cycles.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: As the demand for environmentally friendly energy increasing, types of sustainable energy equipment has been developed in different usage scenarios. Supercapacitors, a potential device for high-power output equipment, have became a research focus around the world. Researchers have developed various materials in order to enhance power density and improve cycling stability [1,2].

Nowadays, researchers have achieved excellent development in different materials for SC electrodes, especially carbon materials, conductive polymers, and transition metal compounds (TMCs). Among transition metal compounds, transition metal hydroxides as an electrode material of SCs have been widely reported due to ease of synthesis, and with reversible reactions between electrode materials and electrolyte ions, transition metal hydroxides have the ability to provide high capacitance.

The cobalt hydroxide (Co(OH)2), as a better candidate in transition metal hydroxides, plays a crucial role in electrochemistry efficiency and operating cost. However, The structure of Co(OH)2 is highly vibrational in electrochemical processes, and, unfortunately, its structural properties and conduction characteristics retard the storage and transportation of charge. To improve the unsatisfactory conductivity, it seems to add materials consisting of different morphologies and textures of carbon is a good way to refine these issues, so graphene [4], carbon nanotubes [5], mesoporous carbons [6] as well as activated carbons couple with Co(OH)2 was developed as active materials. However, compounding with carbon materials of these, there are not enough contact sites for such carbon materials to provide better performance.

Carbon dots, a new type of carbon material, have unique electronic properties and contain kinds of elements, facile doping and functionalization, and provide better solubility in aqueous and nonaqueous solvents [7], which has attracted the attention of the various researcher. It has demonstrated excellent performance as an electrode material when combined with other materials [8–13]. Contribute to the negative charge of carbon dots, and cause the electrostatic interaction between positive metal cations and graphene quantum dots during the synthesis process. However, it can be noticed that pure carbon dots have poor actives when used as electrocatalysts, introducing nitrogen elements into graphene quantum dots can improve the surface activity. Further, form metal-N (Me-N) bond can create a transmission track for the electronic transmission, it also changes crystal growth and obtains much more active spots [13–15].

In the present work, after obtaining nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NQDs), using a facile method to fabricate the Co(OH)2/NQDs composites. The strong connection across NQDs and Co(OH)2 creates new electronic transmission tracks (Co-N), and enhances the electrochemical performances of Co(OH)2/NQDs. The asymmetric supercapacitor equipment synthesized by the Co(OH)2/NQDs composites and GA shows a good energy performance. The hybrid supercapacitor device based on Co(OH)2/NQDs composite and GA shows a high energy density of about 33.6 Wh kg−1 at a power density of 800Wkg−1, and it retains about 137 % specific capacitances after 8000 cycle test. Due to the negative potential of NQDS, it can generate an electrostatic reaction with Co2+, which can stabilize cobalt hydroxide after long cycles.

CA (Citric acid 99.5 %) was acquired from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., ltd (A.R grade) Ethylenediamine was obtained from Shanghai Hushi Laboratorial Equipment Co., ltd. Urea (99 %, A.R). Cobalt Nitrate Hexahydrate (99.99 %) were acquired from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., ltd. Urea (99 %) was also purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., ltd (A.R grade). Conductive carbon black and poly binder purchased from KeJing GROUP. All of the chemicals were used without further purification. N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone was acquired from Aladdin Industrial Corporation.

Nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NQDs) was prepared by hydrothermal method from a previous report [15]. Typically,2.1 g citric acid (CA) and 700 μL ethylenediamine (EDA) was dissolved into 20 ml deionized water and stirred for 10 min, the obtained solution was poured into a 45 ml PPL autoclave and then heated at 210 ◦C for 5 h. After cooling down to room temperature, the obtained solution needs dialysis for 48 h, in order to further purify the NQDs.Then obtained NQDs powders were acquired by further freeze-drying.

The CH was synthesized by the one-pot hydrothermal method. The Co(NO3)2⋅6H2O (0.3 mmol), and urea (3 mmol) were dissolved into 10 ml of deionized water by ultrasonication. And then stirred for 2 h, the obtained solution was moved into a 100 ml Teflon-lined autoclave and then heated in a vacuum oven at 100◦C for 8 h, then, cooling off naturally. The acquired precipitation was centrifugated 3 times at 10000 rpm for 5 min. The purple power was heated at 70 ◦C for 24 h to dry out.

Co(OH)2/NQDs composites were synthesized by a similar method of preparation of Co(OH)2 nanowires. The Co(NO3)2⋅6H2O (0.3 mmol), and urea (3 mmol) were dissolved into 10 ml of deionized water by ultrasonication. different amounts of NQDs were dispersed into the mixed solution. And then stirred for 6 h, the obtained solution was moved into a 100 ml Teflon-lined autoclave and then heated in a vacuum oven at 100◦C for 8 h, then, cooling off naturally. The weight of adding NQDs respectively was 0 mg, 2 mg 4 mg, 8 mg, 16 mg(named as Co(OH)2 and Co(OH)2/NQDs-x, x = 1, 2, 3, 4), respectively.

Graphene oxide was synthesized by the improved Hummer method. The GA was fabricated hydrothermally. The 40 mg Graphene Oxide was added to 10 ml DI to obtain 4 mg/mL GO. After dispersion and sonicated for 2 h, the suspension was sealed in a Teflon-lined autoclave and heated to 180 °C for 16 h. After cooling to room temperature, the sample was washed by DI several times. Then, the graphene aerogel was further freeze-drying for 48 h.

The crystal structures were characterized by using X-ray diffraction (XRD, X’Pert Proxin, Panalytical, Netherlands). The graphitic structure was evaluated by Raman spectroscopy (DXR2Xi, Thermo Fisher Scientific) with a wavelength of 514 nm. The FT-IR spectra were obtained by Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (AVATAR360, Nicolli Instruments). Thermogram was studied using a Synchronous Thermal Analyzer (TG-DTA, STA449C) under airflow. The microstructural morphologies of the samples were obtained by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Supra55, Zeiss, Germany) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, FEI Tecnai F20). The elemental composition was confirmed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, ESCALAB 250). The specific surface area was measured by Nitrogen adsorption/desorption isotherms (3Flex, Micrometric USA). The zeta potential value was tested by zeta potential and nanoparticle size analyzer (NanoPlus3, Micrometric USA).

Electrochemical measurements were put into effect on Chen Hua CHI 760D Electrochemical Workstation. Electrode testing Conditions are in 6.0 M KOH aqueous solution at about 27℃. Using Ag/AgCl electrode as reference electrode, and platinum electrode as reference. For the fabrication of the working electrodes, follow the set weight ratio (8:1:1) to mix active material, conductive carbon black, and poly binder in N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone solvent for about 24 h to obtain a formed slurry. Then, cut cleaned nickel foam into a surface area of 1 cm2, and past obtained formed slurry onto it, and press the foam into thin foil with 10 MPa. After obtaining the electrode, dried it I a vacuum oven at 70 ℃ for 24 h, which is already prepared for further electrochemical testing. By comparing weight before and after preparation, it is about 3 mg mass loaded on nickel foam. In order to constitute the asymmetry supercapacitor device, GA (graphene aerogel) was prepared as anode material. To balance the positive and negative poles, their mass ratio was adjusted by the following equation:
m -
m +
=
C +
Δ V
+
/
C -
Δ V
-
where m is the mass of the positive and negative materials in the electrode, and C (the specific capacitance value)and V (the potential range of the electrode) [16].

As shown in Fig. 1 a, in order to confirm the crystal structures of Co(OH)2/NQDs, XRD measurements were carried out. The peak of NQDs is located at 27°, the broad peak indicates interlayer spacing and a disordered carbon atom. The peaks of pure Co(OH)2 and Co(OH)2/NQDs composite are similar and well matched. The peaks located at 19.9◦, 30.6◦, 35.2◦, 37.8◦, 39.1◦, 41.2◦, 42.5◦,45.9◦, 54.9◦, and 63.0◦. According to the standard card (JCPDS: 30–0443), the lattice fringes of 0.24 nm (101) are attributed to the XRD peaks corresponding to 37.8◦. Attributed to low content in the composite, it is hard to observe the peak of NQDs in the composite [3,17].

Raman results are presented in Fig. 1b. The D band and G band is 1331 cm−1 and 1597 cm−1, respectively, which indicates the existence of graphitic carbon. It is obvious the Raman spectra of Co(OH)2/NQDs-3 composite is also similar to the Raman spectra of pure NQDs, however, the band located at 1085 cm−1 confirms Co(OH)2 exists [18,19].

The result of FT-IR spectra are displayed in Fig. 1. The peak at 1162 cm−1 is index to the asymmetric stretching of C–NH–C. It can be correctly known NQDs contain many nitrogen-containing groups and oxygen-containing groups, which can be used as active spots [20,21]. The stretching vibration of OH and NH bands bring about the approximate result of peaks emerging at 3400 and 2900 cm−1. The vibrational absorption of the CO band contributes to the peak located at 1713 cm−1. Although the curve of Co(OH)2/NQDs-3 is similar to Co(OH)2, the Co-O vibrations can be identified by the peak emerge at 522 cm−1 [22]. Additionally, the curves of Co(OH)2/NQDs have some identical peaks to NQDs. For example, the peak emerges at 1713 cm−1 of the CO band, and the peak is located at 1669 cm−1, which contributes to the vibration of NH.

TG measurement also promotes the combination of Co(OH)2 and NQDs. As shown in Fig. 4d, at about 275℃, the weight of Co(OH)2 and composite decrease sharply. It may cause by the loss of structure water and produced carbon dioxide. After NQDs fed to Co(OH)2, the composite loses more weight, which demonstrates the strong combination of NQDs fed to Co(OH)2. The weight reduction of Co(OH)2 and Co(OH)2/NQDs-3 are 27.14 % and 35.2 %, respectively.

In Fig. 2 . SEM images are introduced to investigate adding different masses of NQDs to Co(OH)2, and the effect to morphologies of Co(OH)2/NQDs composites. The Co(OH)2 needles grow randomly without adding NQDs. With the increased quantity of NQDs, the Co(OH)2/NQDs composites gradually present different characteristics. Co(OH)2/NQDs-1 and Co(OH)2/NQDs-2 reveal a chrysanthemum-like shape, while Co(OH)2/NQDs-3 show more needles on the interface. The feeding mass effect directly the number of needles in one flower. What’s more, the size of Co(OH)2/NQDs composites became smaller with the increased adding mass of NQDs. Commonly, electrode material with a large specific area, would have more available active sites exposed in the electrolyte, which helps electrode material have better wettability. While further increasing the addition amount, the excessive quantity of NQDs could make nanoneedles anchor densely, which changes composites into microspheres. Contribute to the negative charge of NQDs, which causes the electrostatic interaction between Co2+ and NQDs. It may cause the effect the crystal faces growth and change the shape of nanocrystals, like the number of needles increased. The zeta potential value is illustrated in Fig. 2f, which was measured as −12.9 mV. The result is well matched with previous research [22]. The SEM images of GA were displayed in Fig.S3, which was fabricated by hydrothermal. Under hydrothermal reaction, graphene oxide self-assembles into a stable three-dimensional structure.Fig. 3

To figure out the structure and morphology of the Co(OH)2/NQDs-3 composites, TEM measurements and also by high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) were carried out. The interplanar spacing of 0.25 nm is attributed to Co(OH)2, which well matches the literature [3,17]. The layer width of 0.21 nm is attributed to the (100) plane of NQDs [23–26]. In addition, by testing element mapping, it can be observed, that four types of elements (nitrogen, oxygen, carbon, cobalt) are uniform distributed on the surface of Co(OH)2/NQDs-3 composite. The distribution of cobalt and nitrogen is the closest, it may also prove the good combination of cobalt hydroxide and quantum dots. (Fig. 2e-j).

To further study the structure and surface area of Co(OH)2/NQDs composite, Nitrogen adsorption–desorption isotherms was executed. All these above characterization data suggest the success of Co(OH)2/NQDs synthesis. As shown in Fig. S1 This change in shape may cause the change in the exposed active sites. BET analysis is introduced to show the different mass of NQDs fed. The calculated specific surface area of Co(OH)2/NQDs-3 is 21.5331 m2/g, about 1.5 times bigger than pure Co(OH)2 (14.6283 m2/g). However, the geometric specific surface area decreases to 19.0742 m2/g when the additional amount of NQDs is up to 16 mg. Tested the BJH method, the pore size distribution was between 1.738 nm and 56.978 nm. The micropores size are mainly distributed at 1.738 nm, the mesopores are mainly concentrated in the range of around 10 nm. In short, thanks to the open structure, Co(OH)2/NQDs-3 may not just provide more opportunities to come into contact with the electrolyte and boost the number of active spots, but also create a transmission track for electronic transmission. All of these can promote electrochemical performance.

XPS was conducted to investigate the chemical states of Co(OH)2/NQDs. As demonstrated in Fig. 4a, the full scan spectrum indicate the elements the Co(OH)2/NQDs-3 contain are nitrogen, oxygen, carbon, and cobalt, mainly. Compared with pure Co(OH)2, around 400 eV, the signal of the N element can be easily found in Co(OH)2/NQDs-3. Compare to Co(OH)2/NQDs-3, because of no extra N species introduction. There is no signal of N element could be discovered in the wide surface survey of pure Co(OH)2. Regarding the XPS spectra of Co 2p, in Co(OH)2/NQDs-3, the peak located at 781.4 eV is contributed to the Co–N bond, giving evidence that Co species can connect to NQDs by bond formation to transmission electron on Co–N bond (Fig. 4a) [26]. Three peaks are related to pyridinic N, Co-N/pyrrolic N, graphitic N 398 eV, 398.9 eV, and 399.8 eV, respectively. As for the N1s spectra of Co(OH)2/NQDs-3[27,28]. Obviously, in Co(OH)2/NQDs-3, the peak emerges at 399.9 eV relevant to Co–N. The peak shift can be used as a strong proof, revealing the mode of connection between NQDs and Co(OH)2 [31]. Besides, the C 1s spectra of Co(OH)2/NQDs-3 and NQDs were in displayed Fig. S2, four peaks are related to CC/CC, CN, CO, N-CO.
The electrochemical performances of these composites were evaluated by the three-electrode system in 6 M KOH electrolyte.

The cycle voltammetry (CV) was tested at a potential range between 0 V and 0.45 V, it can be noticed, that all CV curves contain oxidation and reduction peaks, which contribute to the pseudocapacitive behavior of Co(OH)2 and Co(OH)2/NQDs. Compare to Co(OH)2/NQDs composites, the CV curve of pure Co(OH)2 is more symmetrical. After adding NQDs, the appearance of cycle voltammetry change slightly.

According to the literature before, it is acknowledge, to describe the redox reactions of Co(OH)2-based materials, following formula are suitable.
3
[ C o
( O H )
3
]
-
↔
Co
3
O 4
+ 4
H 2
O + O
H
-
+ 2
e
-
Co
3
O 4
+ 4
H 2
O + O
H
-
↔ 3 C o O O H +
e
-
CoOOH + O H ↔ C o
O 2
+
H 2
O +
e
-

It can be noticed that the area of the cycle voltammetry for the Co(OH)2/NQDs is much bigger than the area of Co(OH)2. With an increased quantity of NQDs, the area change gradually. Obviously, compared to other Co(OH)2/NQDs composites, it can be observed the Co(OH)2/NQDs-3 own the best performance in all of these materials. The CV curves of different types of materials carried out in 6 M KOH electrolyte at a scan rate of 20 mV s−1 presents in Fig. 4a. And in Fig. 6
, Fig. 7 , the CH and Co(OH)2/NQDs-x composite were all tested at scan rate in the range from 5 mV s−1 to 50 mV s−1. As scan rate change, the CV curves of Co(OH)2 and Co(OH)2/NQDs-4 has slight change, Co(OH)2/NQDs-1, Co(OH)2/NQDs-2, Co(OH)2/NQDs-3 were hardly to observe obvious change, which demonstrates their stability.

To further explore the electrochemical properties of Co(OH)2/NQDs composites, the galvanostatic charge–discharge process was estimated. As the result shown in Fig. 5, the galvanostatic charge–discharge measurements were also executed at the potential range from 0 to 0.45 V. The calculated specific capacitance of the electrodes is at 1A g−1, respectively. The as-obtained Co(OH)2/NQDs-3 composites deliver exhibit capacitance of about 593F g−1 at 1 A g−1. Clearly, the order of the specific capacitance accords fits well with the result of the CV analysis. As Fig. 5 (c) and (d) displayed, the Co(OH)2/NQDs-3 own the best performance in all of these materials, which well matches the result of the cycle voltammetry. The feeding of NQDs provides more active sites, and the strong connection across NQDs and Co(OH)2 creates new electronic transmission tracks (Co-N), which not only improves the conductivity of the material but also creates an open structure to promote the intercalation/deintercalation of ions in the electrolyte solution. When adding amount attain to 16 mg, the performance of Co(OH)2/NQDs-4 composite is significantly reduced, it may contribute to excessive quantum dots causing the Co(OH)2 needles to have a reunion and destroy the favorable structure, which may slow down the intercalation/deintercalation of ions in the electrolyte solution.

As the current densities increase ranging from 1 to 5 A g−1, the discharge time decreases gradually. It can be observed that, though the current densities up to 5 A g−1, the shape of GCD curves almost have no obvious change, which demonstrates good reversibility and rate capability of Co(OH)2/NQDs-3 electrode. In Fig. 5d, the specific capacitance values were presented, which all obtained electrodes were estimated from galvanostatic charge–discharge process. The ion transfer time between the electrode and the electrolyte is insufficient because of increased current densities, all the specific capacitance values descend to high current densities [33]. In order to study the rate capability of the best material, with different scan rates and different current densities cycle voltammetry and galvanostatic charge–discharge measurements were tested, as shown in Fig. 6 and Fig. 5c. Also, the Co(OH)2 and Co(OH)2/NQDs-x composites were all tested at different current densities, which displayed in Fig. 5d and Fig. 8 .Fig. 9

In short, adding a proper amount of NQDs can improve the open structure, and increase specific surface area and spot to promote the intercalation/deintercalation of ions in the electrolyte solution. Co species can connect with NQDs by the bond formation and then transmission electron on Co–N bond, leading to the enhancement of electrochemical performance. However, excessive the additional amount of NQDs cause the needles densely, which is not benefit to the electrochemical performance of Co(OH)2/NQDs composites.

In order to confirm the practical application value of composite materials, a hybrid supercapacitor with Co(OH)2/NQDs as the positive electrode and GA as the negative electrode was fabricated In briefly, and GA was synthesized by the hydrothermal method at 180 °C for 16 h, in order to clarify the structure of GA, the SEM was used. The digital image was shown in Fig. S3. The electrochemical properties of GA were also tested. The electrochemical performances of GA was tested in 6 M KOH electrolyte, which were displayed in Fig. 10 .And the calculated specific capacitance values of GA at a current density of 1 A g−1 is about 203F g−1, the GA also have good reversibility and rate capability.Fig. 11

According to the range of the three-electrode system, the voltage range of the two electrodes are demonstrated from 0 to 1.45 V. And Weight ratio of negative to positive electrode is about 3:1. Fig. S4. shows the CV curves of the Co(OH)2/NQDs//GA hybrid supercapacitor tested at various scan rates, the shape of CV curves almost have no obvious change, which demonstrates good reversibility and rate capability. Fig. 6a and Fig. 6b exhibits the galvanostatic charge/discharge curves of the hybrid supercapacitor device at different current densities, before and after the long cycle test. Fig. 6 is the cycling stability of the device at a current density of 1 A g−1. The specific capacitances intermittent increasing in the first 6000 cycle test, then maintain a stable level. It increased from about 79 F g−1 to about 110F g−1, and it retain about 137 % specific capacitances after the 8000 cycle test.

As observed in the change of electrochemical properties of Co(OH)2/NQDs-3 composites during long cycles test. In order to deeply comprehend crystal changes in the electrochemical test, Co(OH)2/NQDs composites after long cycles tests were tracked by SEM, XPS, which are displayed in Fig. 12 , Fig. 13. , and Fig. 14 . The morphology of composites transfer from chrysanthemum-shaped from nanosheet. Compared with previous studies, it can be observed that the morphology of the material after durability is significantly more stable. Due to the negative potential of NQDs, it can generate an electrostatic reaction with Co2+, which can stabilize cobalt hydroxide after long cycles. XPS may indicate the inserted OH– supplant original anion, which causes the dissolution recrystallization and finally, Co2+ oxidizes to Co3+ and converts into the CoOOH under alkaline electrolyte [29,30]. As for XPS spectra, after a long cycle test, peaks of Co 2p, and O 1 s shift to low binding energy, and both the Co-N and N 1 s intensity have different degrees of reduction, all these demonstrate the Co2+ oxidizes and evolving into high valance state. The result of specific capacitance growth after long cycle test, may attributed to these above reason.

The densities in the range from 1 A g−1 to 5 A g−1 are presented in Fig. 7. This hybrid supercapacitor can deliver an energy density of 33 Wh kg−1 at a power density of 800 Wkg−1. The other devices from recent report present in Fig. 7 and Table S1, for example, CoS//AC [31], Co9S8//AC [32], MnCo2S4//RGO [33], Ni Co-S//AC [34], CuS@CDs-GOH//RGO [35].

In summary, we synthesized nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NQDs) coupled with cobalt hydroxide (Co(OH)2) by a facile hydrothermal method. In the synthesis process, NQDs guide the structure change of cobalt hydroxide (Co(OH)2). When as electrode material three-electrode system, the electrochemical properties of the as-obtained Co(OH)2/NQDs-3 composites is about 593F g−1 at 1 A g−1. The capacitor performance is attributed to the strong coupling between NQDs and Co(OH)2 creates new electron transfer channels (Co-N). The hybrid supercapacitor device synthesized by Co(OH)2/NQDs composite and 3GA delivers a high energy density of about 33 Wh kg−1 at a power density of 800Wkg−1, and it retains about 137 % specific capacitances after 8000 cycle test.

Zihan Chen: Conceptualization, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Weijie Zheng: Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Xiang Zhang: Supervision. Yuying Zheng: Funding acquisition, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jelechem.2022.116666.

ce:section.ce:para:
